item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was liking the article, then I took a gander at the footnotes, and they really elevated the entire article and even made the SCP look more ominious. Easy plus 1 for me, simply because of how clever this entire article was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article puts me in a…weird position, to say the least.

When I first read it one or however long ago it was, I loved this. It was creepy, it was funny, it was interesting and one of the first times I had experienced real narratives in an SCP formatted article. For that, I applaud it for introducing that to me.

However.

I can't like this anymore. The character here is just a cunt. Not even a likable one, or one that I can like to hate. He's just unpleasant. The interview log was funny enough, but it just stopped becoming funny when the joke of "he makes people do stupid things!" got hammered into the ground. The trickster element bores me now, the random references to Author Avatars in such a large concentration were just groaning inducing, and the 'super secret message' thing has been done to death.

Shame, really. I remember this and desperately wanted to love this like I did when I first read it. But I can't. And as such, I downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, what a good article still. To think of all the controversy it caused two and a half years ago, when this is the sort of thing new articles like to do a lot; just be silly and relaxed and ignore site conventions to write good stories. Also, crosslinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read the level 5 clearance message. Holy shit.

Read the last two footnotes. holy SHIT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, so I guessed he'd swapped places with the head researcher as soon as it was mentioned he'd been in that form for a while, but the double-double bluff completely saves it, nice work! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For a sec I thought the O5 was winning. Then I noticed the footnotes. Son of a bitch, who let him do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To clarify: It's supposed to troll people using ctrl+f to look for empty Series 2 slots, much like how the SCP entity itself is a bit of a troll.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh… It's been a while, but the nickname of this article is called [ACCESS DENIED]? What gives?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the bit with Dr. King a lot. His nemesis didn't even show up, but got him nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me go back and upvote 431, but it just seems too "OC"-ish for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I missed the final punchline (for lack of a better word; maybe payoff?) of this entry. I get that it's a trickster, and a well written one, but if there's something beyond that often retreaded type of skip here, and the question of "did it convince the O5s it's contained so it can roam free" I'm not getting it. Maybe I'm dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is criminally underrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article does a lot of things it's not 'supposed' to, including crosslinking, use of author avatars, and hiding containment information. The name is another humorous play on our own format, style, and requirements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"That is the SCP's name."

This might be the only part of the article I don't really "get," now that I think about it. I'm missing the connection between the false-name and the antics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, one of the aspects that prompted a hard upvote was the huge discrepancy between the Caspar and King entries. It took five seconds for me to get Caspar and I seriously laughed out loud, but King…that prank was cruel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait… so the definition of lolfoundation is something that is done for laughs. In your example, there is a deafening silence as to any laughter at all. One might even say it is anti-lolfoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked including them a lot.

I think it's kinda unfortunate that crosslinking gets such a bad rap from people doing it with the big ones, like 682 and 173, especially since the wiki structure we're using means that crosslinking is a natural way to progress from SCP to SCP to Tale to SCP and all around the site. There's too many to just sit down and read now. There need to be signposts saying "This way, please."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People who skim peed their pants. Just be careful…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Likewise. I'm also pleased that 530 and 652 are being allowed visitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Well played Troy, well played. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this article. Rereading it its excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Shirt was subsequently stained" is not something anyone would put in a technical document.

You are incorrect, both in terms of language and in terms of style.

They're obsessively trying to analyze this SCP. Of course they're gonna go with piddly detail that may or may not be relevant.

You're trying to present issues of 'not meshing with your tastes' as objective mistakes. That isn't an argument that can be won in this case, even if it is regularly used to shut down SCPs in lieu of other reasoning elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe not
Think about it

Isn't it odd that the O5 Council is so readily agreeable when it comes to the SCP's new containment protocol? Remember what the object's effects are:

It never offers evidence to support these claims. Rather, people hearing them are simply convinced, usually through what they believe to be completely logical arguments.

Then check out the hidden footnotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see my skip crosslinked there.

I am pleased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, the comments are almost the most interesting part of this article! :O

So, I like this because it's funny and I am horribly disturbed by Dr. King's entry. However, I'm not sure I can totally say I "got it" after reading. The comments helped a lot, so I'm guessing the takeaway is that this thing exists in a perpetual state of "did we trick it?" with the Foundation, which is why they both sacrificed a researcher and are letting it run around uncontained to contain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2062 and 2930 work because their alterations are permanent. Kronecker'd articles can't be changed back unless you move them to another number, and 2930 will change writing about it into the altered form if nobody looks at it for an hour. This is the equivalent of someone vandalizing a Wikipedia article, it can be changed back easily. And I don't think "the Foundation edits altered articles into accurate versions" is headcanon, it's just common sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funny moments =/= lolFoundation, nor do funny moments =/= -J articles. I know this isn't the crux of your argument, but I can't see where this line of thought comes from at all.

What I mean is that it reads like a -J because it is unrealistic. "Shirt was subsequently stained" is not something anyone would put in a technical document.

It's already been shown that the most malevolent thing this guy can do is convince others what it's saying is the truth, or turning into a room for a researcher to walk in on and be defecated out of (I'm honestly surprised you didn't pick that as your example; that'd probably hold more ground). As a sandwich, it can't say anything, nor consume Clef and shit him out. The most malevolent and trollish thing this can do is betray expectations by presuming to be 100% harmless. And, in that form, it IS harmless.

It's a shapeshifter. It could have turned into something far worse without warning. And how is the Foundation supposed to know that its pranks will always be harmless?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you'd like to believe that the Foundation would instantly change the document to its original appearance, that's coming out straight from your headcanon. This article is written by an author holding his own headcanon, and, in his headcanon, it is possible for anomalous objects to modify database entries.

Other articles follow the same premise, like Kronecker and Cross City City City Hall.

And, also, yes, it would make sense as a prank inside the universe.

Dr. John: Hey, can you check 1848 on the database for me?
Dr. Joe: Looks blanked to me.
Dr. John: Oh, fine. I think i got the wrong number. Let me re-check it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To answer this first:

Besides that, there's "shirt was subsequently stained". That reads like something out of a -J.

Funny moments =/= lolFoundation, nor do funny moments =/= -J articles. I know this isn't the crux of your argument, but I can't see where this line of thought comes from at all.

So a shapeshifting, malevolent entity turns into a piece of food, and the Foundation's reaction is to tell an important, probably irreplaceable researcher to go and pick it up? Really?

It's already been shown that the most malevolent thing this guy can do is convince others what it's saying is the truth, or turning into a room for a researcher to walk in on and be defecated out of (I'm honestly surprised you didn't pick that as your example; that'd probably hold more ground). As a sandwich, it can't say anything, nor consume Clef and shit him out. The most malevolent and trollish thing this can do is betray expectations by presuming to be 100% harmless. And, in that form, it IS harmless.

This is why Clef can pick it up. Because not only does Clef have experience to know what to do in dangerous situations anyway, but it's a sandwich, or rather, it's 1848 that reacted to Clef by turning into a sandwich. If someone else went to pick it up, I imagine it would have turned back into something else to fuck with that other person.

Which brings me to my point: none of this shows the Foundation as incompetent or a silly caricature of itself, nor show them being haphazard or act like dumbasses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or, at least, that's what they'd want you to think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that, but why would it decide, in-universe, to make its title "access denied". While it's a hallmark of bad entries out of universe, there's no logical reason for it to consider that a good "prank" in-universe. Also, as I said earlier in the thread, the Foundation would revert the document back to the non-edited version immediately after the entity edited it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lolFoundation is supposed to portray the Foundation as haphazard on safety and rules and regulation and act like dumbasses or do shit an actual working agency of their capacity would never do for laughs.

So a shapeshifting, malevolent entity turns into a piece of food, and the Foundation's reaction is to tell an important, probably irreplaceable researcher to go and pick it up? Really? Besides that, there's "shirt was subsequently stained". That reads like something out of a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clef being in an experiment doesn't automatically make it lolFoundation. Nothing in particular happened. The Foundation researchers were likely perplexed by the reaction, and had Clef pick up the sandwich to see what, if anything, would happen. And nothing in particular did. This is probably the worst example you could've picked, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's because the thing is actually manipulating the file. That's part of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anything, that's the opposite of lolFoundation. Consider the history of Alto Clef on the website and how he's portrayed as this wacky trickster guy who's probably Satan and a badass yet sly and clever or whatever. If you wanted lolFoundation from this, SCP-1848 would probably take on 239, or Agent Strelnikov, or 682, or something ridiculous. lolFoundation would be almost anything else but this. SCP-1848 is a trickster, who messes with the Foundation. Betraying expectations by appearing as a sandwich isn't lolFoundation. It's goddamn anti-climactic.

EDIT: Actually, when I think about it, this isn't even the right usage of lolFoundation. lolFoundation is supposed to portray the Foundation as haphazard on safety and rules and regulation and act like dumbasses or do shit an actual working agency of their capacity would never do for laughs. This doesn't even involve any of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A particularly bad example:

Appeared as an Arby's Roast Beef Sandwich. Dr. Clef was confused, and picked up the sandwich when asked. The sauce on the sandwich dripped free. Dr. Clef's shirt subsequently stained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where's the lolFoundation?

EDIT: To clarify even more, where is the lolFoundation that is so noticeable as to be detrimental to the article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's totally fine. It was one of the main ideas I kept as I was working on it, but I don't think it needs explicit statement in the article itself to make the concept work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What? Why? That doesn't make any sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're welcome to consider that this is just part of his joke. I really love 431 also, but this was just too good an opportunity to pass up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is the SCP's name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By the way, the link on the main list still has [ACCESS DENIED] next to it. You should fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great. You like the piece. I don't.

And yes, I do see the gimmick here. I respectfully disagree that it is particularly original or clever.

So as a counterpoint to what was apparently a perfectly reasonable comment to post on your part:

There's actually not that much going on. It's okay if you don't like it even if someone tells you you're missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Screw you! I'll skim all I want! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seriously, there's so much going on with this one that I'd strongly advise anyone who's neutral/dislikeful toward it to give it a closer re-read until it clicks.

I get the "joke". The O5s have tricked it, except it tricked them, and now it has edited its own documentation. I just don't find it particularly interesting, since it's just a combination of generic and overused shapeshifting and compulsion abilities, is still lolFoundation, and ruins 431 by retconning it.

Also, if it edited its own documentation, it would be quickly changed back. I know that SCPs are supposed to be "windows in time", and don't change as time passes, but this is just ridiculous. Recently, there was a quickly deleted format screw SCP that was supposed to be the documentation submitted by a doctor who had several mental illnesses from exposure to the SCP. The main issue people had with it was that other researchers would so quickly edit the submitted document into a readable form that it killed suspension of disbelief, and that's an issue I have with this too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If 050's not sitting on TroyL's desk after writing this, I'm gonna be severely surprised.

Seriously, there's so much going on with this one that I'd strongly advise anyone who's neutral/dislikeful toward it to give it a closer re-read until it clicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I hate the retconning of 431. Upvoted, but slightly unsatisfied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We do need to use use 'style' and 'tone' to mean what they mean in the real world; otherwise the linguistic drift allows them to mean essentially anything the user wishes, which is confusing for writers who then have to decipher that feedback.

It's like the drift with our accidental redefinition of 'hook' to mean 'literally anything interesting or entertaining in a piece'. The actual meaning of 'hook' is 'something in the beginning of the piece that fascinates the reader and 'hooks' them in'.

I've seen endless confusion and hapless flailing on what people mean by "tone" or "style" or "hook" or all sorts of other words. That's what motivates me to point out the error here.

Tone is the work's attitude towards the subject and the audience. This is the meaning of the word.

Why is it important to talk about it here? Because this is precisely the kind of SCP that ends up getting the shaft because it's different from what we're used to. This page is absolute bait for taste-based critique framed as non-taste-based.

In this case, it won't get the shaft because Troy's an admin and a top writer. Another writer might not be so lucky.

Also, this is more aimed at others reading this page. We already know what you mean because you did clarify, so you're cool.

That said… at least one of those sentences is reasonable confusion. "It is to be heavily guarded" would definitely be bad tone by itself. As it stands, "It is to be heavily guarded, with no fewer than eight regularly rotating guards and a constantly shifting staff" is seriously awkward and should probably just be rephrased.

I probably shouldn't have been so picky when you called that particular sentence "bad tone" — to be honest, that's mostly me being a pedant.

EDIT: Also, a side note. The "tone" of this SCP does include the implied winks to the audience — unusual for a modern SCP article. So yeah there's definitely room to find the tone of the SCP offputting, even if not incorrect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't care to explain to explain "what's going on" because I think the piece is worth more than that, and I still do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the hidden footnotes. Page Source is lovely for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or did they?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The O5 just out-trolled the ur-Troll. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the other way around. The O5 tricked him. We trolled the ur-Troll.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's just style. It's not clinical tone. Tone can't be exchanged with style.

Exchanged? No, not exactly, nor what I intended to imply. But style choices can push something beyond the parameters of clinical tone and these choices do so for me. We can debate the subjectivity of said parameters but that's turtles all the way down and might be a conversation better suited a writing help forum post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't especially helpful. I see this type of comment fairly often, and it doesn't qualify as criticism of the piece. It's criticism of how people are reading the piece. If you're going to tell people they're missing the point, provide some under-the-cut clarification of your viewpoint so we can have an actual discussion about it. This just smacks of "you're doing it wrong, people who downvoted".

I don't care for the tone here, I don't care for the over-extended list of hijinks, the requisite knowledge of staff characters in order to get the (in my opinion) rather bland jokes, and I feel the trickster mythos isn't treated with quite the level of complexity I would expect. That's why I downvoted, and that's my opinion. So if you're going to tell me I'm missing something integral, shed a little light on your own opinion why this rises above the interpretations of others, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As cool of a concept as that is, I gotta agree with Moose. All I got out of it were annotations. Not once did I really ever pick up that it was most of the article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (I actually disliked them but totally forgot to mention that fact >_>;;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Appreciated! TL;DR — yes, all those examples are stylistic choices, and acceptable tone.

Details under the cut
Hide

It's a relative word with no real context here and groks as not very clinical.

That's not correct. It's acceptable tone because the rest of the sentence explains what is meant by 'heavily'. (It WOULD be bad tone if the rest of the sentence wasn't there; as it stands, it's just style.)

if it's a stylistic choice then I guess we can agree to disagree but I'd say most of these instances would read with a more clinical tone if broken into smaller sentences.

That's just style. It's not clinical tone. Tone can't be exchanged with style.

It feels unclear whether "basic" here is actual basic amenities or requests of more creature-comfort-y items.

In this case it doesn't particularly matter, because it's a summary and conveying non-vital information (and therefore acceptable tone).

Saying the requests are "carefully analyzed" also seems unnecessary.

Sure, but again, this is a style thing.

"Simply" is another case similar to "heavily" for me.

No — because in this case, it means exactly what it says and doesn't require elaboration. That makes it style, not tone.

Hide

(Though once again, disliking stylistic choices is fine.)
